Attachment → artifact → model

One diagram of custody, persistence, workspace copies, multiple files, direct API behavior, and later access. The channel provider is transport; artifact storage is the source of truth.

Deployment status: the implementation passes local checks, but Convex codegen is still required before production deployment.

1. Where attachments enter

Durable flow implemented

Telegram / Slack / Pancake

The server automatically reads authenticated channel attachments. There is no attachment command.

Current turn only

POST SSE / async / WebSocket

User events accept inline base64 or data-URL image and file parts. HTTP media URLs are rejected.

Not implemented

Direct artifact upload/reference API

Direct inline bytes are not yet promoted into durable artifact storage, materialized, or available for later rehydration.

2. Channel attachment custody

Temporary authority

Channel provider

Telegram, Slack, or Pancake exposes a temporary media location.

Exactly once

Guarded download

DNS pinned, redirects checked, credentials scoped, timeout and byte limits enforced.

Trust boundary

Validate

Safe filename, MIME sniffing, active-content rejection, size and SHA-256.

Transfer only

Private staging S3

Short-lived handoff copy. It is deleted after remote storage succeeds.

Configured on the agent

Developer remote artifact driver

Core sends a signed store lifecycle request. The developer stores bytes in their S3, R2, GCS, database, or service and returns an opaque reference.

No driver configured

Managed ephemeral artifact storage

The private staging object remains the source of truth, but is eligible for deletion after one day. This is temporary, not durable storage.

Where exactly does the driver sit?

After shared validation and staging, before the artifact becomes ready. It never receives the channel provider URL or channel token. Later, core calls the same driver’s resolve operation to obtain a fresh short-lived read capability, downloads through the guarded client, and verifies the original size and SHA-256.

3. One message can create many artifacts

Per attachment

Artifact control records

Each accepted file gets its own artifact ID, state, filename, MIME, size, checksum, driver ID, and opaque storage reference.

artifact Aartifact Bartifact C… up to 8
Model limits

Multiple model inputs

All supported current-turn parts can be projected. On later turns, multiple distinct artifacts can be rehydrated, each at most once, within the shared 20 MiB invocation budget.

8 inbound items max20 MiB aggregate

4. After storage: workspace, history, and model are separate

Optional working copy

Writable workspace

Core copies from artifact storage, never from the channel provider, into .artifacts/<artifactId>/<filename>. It does not extract or execute the file.

Persistent metadata only

Conversation history

Stores artifact ID, type, size, checksum, filename, and optional workspace path. No bytes, provider URL, signed URL, or opaque driver reference.

Request-local bytes

Model projection

Current turn receives each explicitly supported MIME. Later turns use the scoped artifact tool to reload and integrity-check selected artifacts.

No workspace or read-only workspace

Nothing is lost. Bytes remain in the remote driver or managed ephemeral S3. Native model-supported files can still be projected; complex processing remains unavailable.

Multiple writable workspaces

No ambiguous write occurs. The agent must configure the artifact workspace name; otherwise the artifact remains stored without a workspace copy.

5. Scope and driver revision behavior

Account scoped

Driver config

Stored in an agent’s encrypted configuration and used only by that agent runtime.

Agent referenced

Workspace

The workspace record is account-owned and may be shared by agents referencing the same workspace ID.

Conversation scoped

Artifact access

Reads require the same account and conversation. Knowing another artifact ID is insufficient.

Current limitation

No driver revision

The record stores only driver name plus opaque reference, not an immutable endpoint/config revision.

What happens when driver configuration changes?
  • Same name, compatible backend: later reads continue if the service can resolve old opaque references.
  • Name removed or renamed: historical artifacts become unavailable because core cannot select their driver.
  • Same name, different backend: an old opaque reference may be sent to the wrong service. This is why driver identity should become an immutable resource ID plus revision.
  • Ingress retry with a different name: now fails closed rather than silently moving the existing artifact.

6. Current scenarios

InputStatusResult
Channel image/file/videoDurable flowValidated, stored through driver or managed ephemeral, descriptor persisted, capability-aware projection.
Several channel attachmentsSupportedIndependent artifact records and partial failure handling; up to eight items and 20 MiB aggregate.
POST SSE / async / WebSocket inline image/fileCurrent turn onlyPassed to the model and removed from history; not yet stored as an artifact.
Remote URL in direct APIRejectedPrevents arbitrary server-side URL fetching. A future authenticated artifact import API must own that boundary.
No writable workspaceArtifact retainedRemote or managed storage remains source of truth; native projection still works where supported.
ZIP, OCR, transcription, extractionDeferred processingMay materialize, but no automatic extraction or execution occurs.
Transport/model boundary Artifact source of truth Temporary or working copy Partial/current-turn behavior Not implemented
